objective: Test whether Phase 1 ZDTP 98.7% on Riemann zero positions is discriminating vs. synthetic controls in same range

zdtp.gateway_ordering: S1 > S2 > S3A > S3B = S4 > S5 (monotone descent)

control_a_growth_noise.note: Sub-range artifact: sequence peaks at ~33, not ~67 — range mismatch with actual zeros

zdtp.gateway_ordering: S2 > S1 (S1 lead disrupted by noise)

control_b_poisson_gaps.note: Compressed range, slow spread — different endpoint than actual zeros

control_c_uniform.note: Perfectly deterministic, same range as actual first 16 zeros

zdtp.gateway_ordering: S1 > S2 monotone descent — same pattern as actual zeros

summary_table.zdtp_verdict: NON-DISCRIMINATING — all four sequences score within noise band
summary_table.chavez_verdict: NON-DISCRIMINATING at n=16 — sample too small for meaningful bilateral analysis
summary_table.gateway_ordering_verdict: WEAKLY DISCRIMINATING — S1 monotone descent shared by actual zeros and uniform control only; noisy/Poisson controls show disrupted ordering

F1_zdtp_non_discriminating.answer: ZDTP convergence at n=16 is NOT discriminating for Riemann-specific structure
F1_zdtp_non_discriminating.significance: Any monotone sequence in this range scores ~97-99%. The Phase 1 98.7% result was a range/monotonicity artifact, not evidence of Riemann-specific algebraic structure. ZDTP is appropriate for regime detection, not 16-element sequence characterization.

F2_gateway_ordering_weak_signal.answer: Gateway ordering (S1 monotone descent) distinguishes smooth from noisy sequences
F2_gateway_ordering_weak_signal.significance: Actual zeros and Control C (uniform) share S1-lead monotone descent. Controls A (noise) and B (Poisson) show disrupted ordering. This is a smoothness signal, not a Riemann-specific signal.

F3_chavez_n16_insufficient.answer: Chavez symmetry at n=16 is insufficient — actual zeros score lowest of the group (60.1%)
F3_chavez_n16_insufficient.significance: The Phase 1 83.8% result was on 99 gap values. The n=16 position test is underpowered. Minimum useful n for Chavez analysis appears to be ~50-100 data points.

F4_phase2_reframe_triggered.answer: Phase 2 reframe triggered per handoff document criterion
F4_phase2_reframe_triggered.significance: Controls B and C both exceeded 98.7%. The real discriminability test must operate on the gap sequence (n=99), not positions (n=16). Reframed Phase 2 tests: shuffle test on actual gaps, synthetic Poisson gap sequences at n=99, scale invariance check across gap sample sizes.

F5_meaningful_phase1_result.answer: The meaningful Phase 1 result is Chavez conjugation symmetry 83.8% on 99 gaps
F5_meaningful_phase1_result.significance: This measurement uses sufficient n, operates on gaps (removing monotone growth artifact), and placed Riemann zero gaps squarely in the GUE/structured-prime range (78-86%). That result stands. The ZDTP 98.7% on positions does not.

reframed_phase2_next_steps: Shuffle test: shuffle actual 99 zero gaps, re-run Chavez; if symmetry drops from 83.8%, gap ordering carries structure
Synthetic gap control: 99 Poisson gaps with mean 1.41, run Chavez; compare to actual 83.8%
Scale invariance: Chavez symmetry on first 100, 500, 999 gaps — does 83.8% hold?
AT-1 literature check: Biss-Dugger-Isaksen before annihilation topology Phase 1
ZDTP corrected application: run on gap sequence at n=99 (not positions at n=16)
